turns.name: JOHN G. ROBERTS, JR.
turns.text: Justice Kavanaugh has the opinion of the Court this morning in two cases.

turns.name: BRETT M. KAVANAUGH
turns.text: The first opinion I have to announce is No. 23-1226, McLaughlin versus McKesson. This case involves the Telephone Consumer Protection Act of 1991 or TCPA. The TCPA protects businesses and consumers from intrusive telemarketing communications. Among other restrictions, the TCPA prohibits a business from sending an unsolicited advertisement by fax absent an opt-out notice informing recipients that they can choose not to receive future faxes. McKesson Corporation is a healthcare company. In 2009 and 2010, in an effort to promote McKesson's products, McKesson sent unsolicited fax advertisements to various medical practices. McLaughlin Chiropractic Associates received some of those faxes. McLaughlin later sued McKesson. McLaughlin alleged that McKesson violated the TCPA by faxing unsolicited advertisements without the opt-out notice that the statute requires. The TCPA sets damages at $500.00 for each unsolicited fax sent without an opt-out notice. McLaughlin requested damages and it also sought to represent a class of other recipients of McKesson's faxes and make this a class action lawsuit. That class action point is important because class actions alleging TCPA violations can turn out to involve millions of dollars in claim damages given the $500.00 per fax and statutory damages and given the number of unsolicited faxes that businesses sometimes would send to different potential customers without opt-out notices. Here, some recipients of the faxes including McLaughlin had received faxes on a traditional fax machine, the standalone device dedicated to receiving and printing faxes, but others had received the faxes through online fax services, either by email or through an online portal. The District Court certified a class of fax recipients drawing no distinction between faxes received on traditional fax machines and faxes received through online fax services. As McLaughlin's lawsuit progressed, another company with no connection to the litigation petitioned the Federal Communications Commission for declaratory ruling about whether the TCPA actually applies to faxes received through online fax services as distinct from those received through stand-alone fax machines. Months after the District Court certified the class in McLaughlin suit, the FCC issued an order known as the Amerifactors Order, ruling that the TCPA does not prohibit those faxes sent through online fax services. As the parties here recognized, if the FCC's Amerifactors Order were binding on the District Court in this litigation, it would undermine McLaughlin's class action lawsuit, because McLaughlin defined the class to include plaintiffs who received unsolicited faxes through online fax services. After receiving briefing on the issue, the District Court deemed the FCC's Amerifactors Order a final binding order that dictated the Court's interpretation of the TCPA. On appeal, the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit agreed that the District Court was bound by the FCC's Amerifactors Order. This Court granted review to decide whether the District Court was required to follow the FCC's legal interpretation of the TCPA. As explained at some length in the Court's opinion today, we conclude that the answer is no, and we therefore disagree with the Court of Appeals and the District Court. District Courts in enforcement proceedings like this are not bound by an agency's interpretation of the relevant statute. Here, therefore, the District Court should interpret the TCPA as courts traditionally do under ordinary principles of statutory interpretation affording appropriate respect to the FCC's interpretation, but without being bound by the FCC's interpretation. In sum, we reverse the judgment of the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit. Justice Kagan has filed the dissenting opinion which Justice Sotomayor and Justice Jackson have joined.
